# Supply Chain Visibility: 5 Digital Solutions to Put the Puzzle Together

![](https://dfifnluhfzgk8.cloudfront.net/wp-content/uploads/2023/09/Volodymyr-Horovyi.png?format=webp&quality=100)
![](https://dfifnluhfzgk8.cloudfront.net/wp-content/uploads/2023/05/daria-iaskova.jpeg?format=webp&quality=100)

*Visibility* is a key component of success in modern supply chains—it allows managers to get real-time status updates, track important KPIs, and improve operational efficiency.

*Visibility* makes a huge part in resilience, as it helps companies spot and reduce risks and overcome supply chain disruptions.

*Visibility* is also a leap towards customer satisfaction, as today consumers expect goods to be delivered within the shortest time possible. And often, it’s not even about days, it’s about hours.

On their way to building smarter supply chains, [92%](https://www.mckinsey.com/capabilities/operations/our-insights/how-covid-19-is-reshaping-supply-chains) of global companies make their investments in supply chain visibility and become market leaders. What tools and technologies help them achieve success and solve the visibility puzzle? Let’s explore this in detail.

## What is supply chain visibility and why is it important?

Supply Chain Visibility (SCV) is a process that encompasses **control over each component of a supply chain**—strategic planning, delivery, returns, and everything in-between. It provides a **transparent view of every supply chain tier**—from the raw materials to the customer.

### Types of supply chain visibility

Most often, speaking of supply chain visibility, we refer to a real-time view of aggregated data coming from suppliers, manufacturers, and [transportation and logistics service providers](https://www.trinetix.com/industries/logistics) through integrated supply chain solutions (ERP, WMS, TMS). In this case, types of visibility in the supply chain boil down to understanding transportation, shipment, and supply chain processes.

On one hand, types of supply chain visibility vary from company to company—and that’s justified by the uniqueness of operations and business processes within different organizations. But on the other hand, the **need for improved transparency is usually the strongest in the four supply chain areas**.

![components-of-supply-chain visibility](https://dfifnluhfzgk8.cloudfront.net/wp-content/uploads/2023/07/components-of-supply-chain-visibility.svg)

### Bottom line impact of supply chain visibility

For the past few years, visibility has deservedly become the king of supply chain management.

Companies that prioritize end-to-end supply chain visibility report getting several game-changing advantages. Among them are **data-driven decision-making, decreased supply chain disruptions, increased operational and business efficiency, and improved customer satisfaction**.

Often, the importance of supply chain visibility is explained with the four C’s: *complexity, customers, compliance, and competitiveness*.

![supply-chain-visibility-scv](https://dfifnluhfzgk8.cloudfront.net/wp-content/uploads/2023/07/supply-chain-visibility-scv.svg)

**Complexity** refers to the diversity and scale of suppliers’ and partners’ networks that make up a supply chain. Here visibility helps to prevent various logistics issues and as a result, reduces reputational risks, and improves profitability.

**Customers** expect to have their favorite products available at specific locations of interest. Otherwise, they will remain dissatisfied and find other goods or service providers. Real-time supply chain visibility helps businesses deliver goods quickly and keeps customers updated on their orders.

**Compliance** is a vital component of global supply chains. With a number of trade agreements, procurement rules, and government tariffs, maintaining compliance can become an issue. By building visibility into compliance and regulatory requirements companies demonstrate their commitment to ethical practices, risk mitigation, and the protection of stakeholders' interests.

**Competitiveness** is another benefit organizations are getting from supply chain visibility—it helps businesses spot inefficiencies throughout the whole supply chain, reduce operational costs, and as a result, outpace competitors.

## Key challenges of supply chain visibility

According to a BDO survey, only [42%](https://insights.bdo.com/2023-BDO-Manufacturing-CFO-Outlook-Survey.html) of manufacturers stated their intention to improve supply chain visibility, while the rest of respondents didn’t consider SCV their priority. The result raises a logical question: if visibility in supply chain is so important, why don’t organizations prioritize it more?

The issue lies not with the visibility itself, but with the number of challenges manufacturers face. The path to clarity is never an easy one, so to achieve great visibility supply chain needs to overcome the following obstacles:

As it can be seen, most supply chain visibility challenges ironically stem from the lack of visibility. To get a better view of their supply chain, organizations need to remove issues and inaccuracies. And yet, without better visibility supply chain managers are unable to timely detect and address these problems. However, there is a way out of an apparent dead end, presented by supply chain visibility software.

## The role of supply chain visibility technology in supply chain transformation

While the role of supply chain visibility is clear, building it in practice may already seem a puzzle, solving which requires much time and effort. And to some part, it’s true – as gaining a holistic view into large supply chain structures can’t be simple by nature. But here we approach the most exciting part of building a clearer view of supply chain management—types of supply chain visibility software companies can use to gain full control over all stages of goods transportation.

### AI for next-gen supply chain visibility and analytics

*Implementing AI has helped businesses improve logistics costs by 15%, inventory levels by 35%, and service levels by 65%.* ***McKinsey***

Today [AI empowers a number of supply chain areas](https://www.trinetix.com/insights/the-advancing-role-of-ai-in-logistics-and-supply-chains), including inventory planning, route optimization, demand forecasting, sales and marketing, and fault analysis. But in addition to rethinking logistics operations from the inside, AI transforms the outer side of supply chain management offering game-changing visibility enhancements.

By considering multiple factors and scenarios, AI helps supply chain managers make informed decisions regarding order fulfillment, inventory replenishment, supplier selection, and demand management—providing companies with a completely new and smart decision system for supply chain visibility and analytics.

![](https://dfifnluhfzgk8.cloudfront.net/wp-content/uploads/2024/04/AI-logistics_Preview.png?format=webp)

### Blockchain for establishing transparency and trust

*The global blockchain supply chain market is expected to reach**$9.85 billion by 2025.* ***Allied Market Research***

The decentralized nature of blockchain and smart contracts that are part of the technology, make it a perfect fit for improving essential supply chain processes like invoice management, freight monitoring, product-trace-and-track, and information processing.

The technology is actively used and positively valued by supply chains global leaders like Maersk and retail giants like Walmart. Let’s explore how [blockchain in supply chains](https://www.trinetix.com/insights/blockchain-in-supply-chains-empowering-transparency-and-traceability) can help companies power up resilience and build end-to-end visibility.

As a result, supply chain visibility technology [powered by blockchain](https://www.trinetix.com/services/blockhain-software-development) instills customer confidence by providing them with the opportunity to verify product authenticity and track their purchases in real-time.

### Intelligent automation for enhanced efficiency and productivity

*75% of warehouses would adopt automation solutions by the end of 2023.****Deloitte and MHI***

The abundance of manual processes, legacy flows, and routine tasks that characterize supply chain management make the industry a perfect playground for leveraging [intelligent automation](https://www.trinetix.com/services/intelligent-automation).

And the scale of automation is impressive, as it literally encompasses all stages and areas of logistics management—from document digitization to warehouse stock monitoring.

[Logistics process automation](https://www.trinetix.com/insights/automation-in-logistics-and-warehousing-new-performance-frontier), including robotic process automation (RPA), contributes to supply chain visibility in a number of ways.

![](https://dfifnluhfzgk8.cloudfront.net/wp-content/uploads/2023/09/Volodymyr-Horovyi.png?format=webp)

In the long run, automating repetitive and manual tasks frees up time for supply chain professionals to focus on strategic activities. This improved efficiency and productivity allow them to dedicate more resources to monitoring, analyzing, and optimizing supply chain processes, leading to better supply chain visibility and performance.

### Logistics customer portal software: meeting consumers’ expectations

*90% of customers want immediate updates and real-time visibility on their orders.* ***DispatchTrack***

Visibility is as essential for B2B and B2C companies as for their customers, whose demand for the quality of services constantly increases. To meet this demand, companies are increasingly bringing real-time order tracking capabilities implemented as a part of a [customer logistics portal](https://www.trinetix.com/insights/essential-components-of-logistics-customer-portal-software). Here's how this type of supply chain visibility solutions can facilitate and streamline operations.

By providing customers with a dedicated space for order monitoring, logistics customer portal software enhances supply chain visibility by offering real-time tracking, document access, communication, order management, exception handling, analytics, and self-service capabilities. This results in streamlined processes and enhanced overall visibility throughout the supply chain and improved customer satisfaction.

### Logistics dashboards for unprecedented visibility and growth

*As of 2023, 67% of business leaders claim that dashboards help them improve end-to-end supply chain visibility.* ***McKinsey***

[Logistics dashboards](https://www.trinetix.com/insights/empowering-smarter-supply-chains-with-logistics-dashboards) provide a visual representation of critical data related to logistics operations and allow supply chain businesses efficiently track their key performance indicators (KPIs). In fact, dashboards build a direct correlation between supply chains and visibility.

By offering centralized, real-time data visualization, performance monitoring, exception management, and collaboration capabilities, logistics dashboards enhance visibility across the supply chain. Such supply chain visibility solutions provide stakeholders with actionable insights, enabling them to make informed decisions, identify areas for improvement, and drive operational excellence.

## Supply chain visibility technology: making the puzzle come together

The variety of modern digital tools and technologies make lack of visibility in supply chain less of an issue, turning into a manageable task. Moreover, using the tech essentials we mentioned earlier allows companies to boost their operational efficiency, establish trust with consumers and scale their business.

At the same time, implementing supply chain visibility solutions requires a strategic approach and the involvement of highly qualified subject matter experts.

# What are the challenges in supply chain visibility & how can technology solve them?

Blog

By Komal Puri | October 13, 2022

Poor supply chain visibility has been choking the supply chain and logistics industry for decades. According to SupplyChainDive, 94% of businesses do not have full visibility of their supply chain and logistics operations. The problems that come with poor supply chain visibility gradually eats into a business’ revenue, efficiency, productivity and customer experience.

The question is why is it so difficult to achieve high levels of supply chain visibility? Traditional means of executing operations is one of the major reason behind poor visibility. Legacy IT systems work in silos. This makes it extremely difficult for supply chain and logistics stakeholders to get a holistic picture of all that’s happening on the ground. Before we delve deeper into the problems let us quickly understand what supply chain visibility really means.

## What is supply chain visibility?

Supply chain visibility refers to core logistics activities like in-transit movement of goods or freight, in-plant logistics activities, movement of delivery vehicles from warehouses to hubs to the customer's doorstep, movement of trucks from manufacturing units to end-customers, being 100% transparent, trackable and traceable to supply chain stakeholders.

In the case of traditional supply chain and logistics management, for instance, when an order moves from a warehouse to a central hub for final dispatch to customers, there is little or no in-transit visibility of those orders. This happens because the warehouse management systems (WMS) and the systems being used by 3PL logistics providers are not interoperable. Similarly, a transport management system (TMS) is not in sync with an inventory management system, an order management system (OMS) lacks interoperability with TMS, so on and so forth. Poor visibility results in a series of inefficiencies within a supply chain.

## **Top four challenges that come with a lack of visibility**

Since customer expectations are at stake, smart businesses are addressing the [poor visibility](https://fareye.com/resources/blogs/shipment-visibility-platform) challenge head-on. Streamlining processes, integrating operations and improving connectivity are the main areas that will empower enterprises to gain high levels of operational visibility. Businesses will need to look for ways to integrate disparate logistics systems like TMS, WMS, OMS, 3PL systems and more to ensure supply chain visibility.

### Technologies That Can Address Supply Chain Visibility Challenges

Savvy businesses are also aggressively exploring and investing in cutting-edge technologies like machine learning, business analytics and digital control towers to enhance [supply chain and logistics visibility](https://fareye.com/resources/blogs/logistics-visibility-software).

### Machine Learning

Machine learning algorithms empower businesses to gain predictive intelligence by scanning through historical KPI-data of routes previously undertaken by 3PL providers. Knowledge about these KPIs empowers logistics stakeholders to gain predictive visibility.

### Business/Cognitive Analytics

Business or cognitive analytics empower businesses to mine and crunch data from disparate systems and gain critical business insights. It helps logistics stakeholders accurately benchmark KPIs, figure out inconsistencies in SLAs, identify financial holes and other inconsistencies in the entire process of supply chain and logistics.

### Digital Control Tower

A digital control tower (DCT) empowers businesses to gain real-time supply chain visibility that logistics stakeholders can act upon such as pick-up problems, route-wise performance, delivery delays for all orders on a single screen, drastically improving supply chain management.

As customer experience rapidly takes over price as a major competitive differentiator and margins keep shrinking, ensuring end-to-end visibility of supply chain and logistics operations will become imperative. There is no way out but to integrate disparate IT-systems and embrace disruptive technologies.

# Overcoming Common Challenges in Supply Chain Visibility

## Table of content

![Overcoming Common Challenges in Supply Chain Visibility](https://sinay.ai/wp-content/uploads/2025/01/common-challenges-1024x512.jpg)

Imagine assembling a puzzle, where each piece must fit perfectly to create a complete picture. In the shipping industry, the [supply chain](https://sinay.ai/en/maritime-glossary/supply-chain/) operates in the same way every stage, from procurement to transportation to delivery, must align seamlessly to ensure timely, efficient deliveries. According to a Deloitte report, **79% of companies with high-performing supply chains experience revenue growth above their industry peers.** However, many businesses still face challenges with visibility and data integration.

This article will explore these common obstacles and offer strategies and technological solutions to enhance in [Supply Chain](https://sinay.ai/en/maritime-glossary/supply-chain/) Visibility (SCV) and optimize operations.

## Understanding Supply Chain Visibility and Its Importance

With consumers demanding faster deliveries, real-time tracking, and constant updates on their orders, customer expectations have reached unprecedented levels. Companies without strong SCV policies risk falling behind, as delays or lack of communication can rapidly damage customer trust and loyalty.

### Importance of Supply Chain Visibility

[Supply chain visibility](https://sinay.ai/en/unlocking-the-power-of-supply-chain-visibility/) began to take shape in the 1980s and 1990s with the rise of technologies like barcoding and early inventory management systems. However, it wasn’t until the early 2000s, with the advent of more advanced technologies that SCV gained significant traction. **SCV refers to the ability to track, monitor, and manage the movement of goods, inventory, and information across the entire [supply chain](https://sinay.ai/en/maritime-glossary/supply-chain/) in real time.** It plays a critical role in modern supply chains by giving businesses the insights to make informed decisions, improve efficiency, and ensure compliance with industry standards. The ability to trace goods from the source to the customer is crucial. Its importance can be highlighted in the following ways:

Improved Decision-Making: SCV provides instant data that helps businesses make informed decisions, optimizing routes, inventory, and resource allocation.

Enhanced Inventory Management: With better visibility into inventory levels, companies can reduce stockouts and overstocking and boost overall inventory efficiency.

Better Customer Service: Real-time tracking and updates allow companies to provide accurate delivery estimates and strengthen transparency, boosting [customer satisfaction](https://sinay.ai/en/the-link-between-supply-chain-visibility-and-customer-satisfaction/).

Risk Management and Compliance: It helps identify risks and ensures compliance with regulations, enabling businesses to address potential disruptions and maintain industry standards.

### Common Challenges in Achieving Supply Chain Visibility

A 2024 report highlighted that only **6% of companies have achieved full [supply chain](https://sinay.ai/en/maritime-glossary/supply-chain/) visibility**. Many companies face significant challenges in gaining transparency across their [supply chain](https://sinay.ai/en/maritime-glossary/supply-chain/) due to issues like fragmented data, lack of integration, and inadequate technology. Common challenges in achieving [supply chain](https://sinay.ai/en/maritime-glossary/supply-chain/) visibility include:

Data Fragmentation and Integration: Many firms struggle with fragmented data across various systems, making it difficult to integrate and obtain a clear view of the entire [supply chain](https://sinay.ai/en/maritime-glossary/supply-chain/).

Inadequate Technology: Outdated or incompatible technology can limit the ability to track goods instantly, impacting product quality and operational efficiency.

Collaboration with Suppliers: Effective SCV requires close cooperation with suppliers to ensure data accuracy and timely information sharing. A lack of cooperation or transparency from suppliers can lead to delays, affecting customer demand and overall business performance.

Data Accuracy and Quality: Poor-[quality data](https://sinay.ai/en/the-importance-of-quality-data-in-container-shipping/) can lead to errors in tracking and decision-making, undermining the effectiveness of SCV systems. Ensuring accurate and consistent data across all [supply chain](https://sinay.ai/en/maritime-glossary/supply-chain/) stages is essential to meeting customer demand and optimizing cost.

### Technological Solutions for Facilitating Supply Chain Transparency

As Margrethe Vestager, the European Commissioner for Competition, once said, “Technology shapes our world, but we must ensure it serves the people and not the other way around.” In the shipping industry, transformative technologies are shaping [supply chain](https://sinay.ai/en/maritime-glossary/supply-chain/) transparency, empowering businesses with real-time data, secure information sharing, and greater operational efficiency. Here are some of the key technologies revolutionizing [supply chain](https://sinay.ai/en/maritime-glossary/supply-chain/) transparency:

Internet of Things (IoT) Devices: IoT devices, such as sensors and [GPS trackers](https://sinay.ai/en/the-role-of-gps-in-modern-container-tracking/), provide **instant data on the location and condition of goods** throughout the [supply chain](https://sinay.ai/en/maritime-glossary/supply-chain/). By embedding sensors in shipments or equipment, firms can monitor temperature, humidity, and even the movement of goods, enabling proactive judgment and reducing the risk of delays or product degradation.

Radio Frequency Identification ([RFID](https://sinay.ai/en/maritime-glossary/rfid/)): The technology uses electromagnetic fields to **identify and track** tags attached to products. This provides users with accurate, instant information on inventory levels, item locations, and movement across various stages of the [supply chain](https://sinay.ai/en/maritime-glossary/supply-chain/). [RFID](https://sinay.ai/en/maritime-glossary/rfid/) eliminates manual scanning, streamlining operations, and ensuring that data is consistently updated.

Artificial Intelligence: AI algorithms can analyze vast amounts of data from various sources in real-time to **predict [supply chain](https://sinay.ai/en/maritime-glossary/supply-chain/) disruptions, optimize routes, and boost inventory management.** Machine learning models can help businesses [forecast](https://sinay.ai/en/maritime-glossary/forecast/) demand more accurately, automate evaluation, and identify inefficiencies, making the [supply chain](https://sinay.ai/en/maritime-glossary/supply-chain/) more agile and responsive to market changes.

Blockchain: The advancement offers a decentralized and immutable ledger that ensures **transparency and security** in data sharing across the [supply chain](https://sinay.ai/en/maritime-glossary/supply-chain/). Each transaction or movement of goods is recorded in a secure, tamper-proof manner, allowing all stakeholders from suppliers to customers to access accurate, up-to-date information. Blockchain enhances trust and reduces fraud, providing an auditable trail of the product journey.

![Technological Solutions for Supply Chain Transparency](https://sinay.ai/wp-content/uploads/2025/01/transparency-1024x512.jpg)

## Overcoming Supply Chain Visibility Challenges

With increasing demands for real-time tracking, accurate data sharing, and seamless collaboration, companies must address obstacles including fragmented systems, lack of transparency, and technological gaps.

### Strategies for Improving Visibility in the Supply Chain

How can businesses overcome the challenges of [supply chain](https://sinay.ai/en/maritime-glossary/supply-chain/) visibility in an increasingly complex and fast-paced environment? To tackle issues, companies must adopt effective strategies that enhance transparency and c partnership.

Implement Real-Time Tracking Systems

One of the most effective strategies for improving [supply chain](https://sinay.ai/en/maritime-glossary/supply-chain/) visibility is the use of live tracking systems. These solutions provide businesses with **up-to-the-minute information on the location, status, and condition of shipments,** helping to eliminate delays and reduce uncertainty.

Enhance Data Collaboration Across Stakeholders

This involves using technologies and systems that enable real-time access to accurate data, ensuring that everyone has the same information simultaneously. Integrating cloud-based systems and standardized data exchange protocols can further **enhance partnerships, reducing errors and miscommunication.** This elevated transparency helps identify issues early, optimize operations, and foster stronger, more efficient partnerships across the entire [supply chain](https://sinay.ai/en/maritime-glossary/supply-chain/).

Build Centralized Platforms for Data Access

# Supply Chain Transparency vs. Supply Chain Visibility

While the terms "supply chain visibility" and "supply chain transparency" are often used interchangeably, they represent distinct concepts that serve complementary purposes.

**Supply chain visibility** refers to the ability to establish clarity and insights into every stage of the supply chain. This includes tracking raw materials, manufacturing sites, suppliers, and the journey to the end market. Supply chain visibility is about creating a comprehensive view of suppliers and goods for the purposes of identifying risks, inefficiencies, and opportunities.

**Supply chain transparency,** on the other hand, involves sharing this information with consumers and stakeholders in a way thatâs both meaningful and actionable. Transparency takes the data provided by visibility and transforms it into a narrative that builds trust, meets regulatory requirements, and aligns with stakeholder expectations.

For example, supply chain visibility might uncover the carbon footprint of a product across its lifecycle. Supply chain transparency would then communicate this information to stakeholdersâperhaps as part of an ESG report or a sustainability pledgeâin a manner that resonates and informs.

In essence, visibility is about understanding the supply chain, while transparency is about sharing that information effectively. Together, they form the foundation of modern supply chain management, where both insight and communication are vital to building resilient, responsible, and trusted operations.

## The Benefits of Supply Chain Visibility

The [benefits of supply chain visibility](https://www.z2data.com/insights/why-supply-chain-visibility-is-imperative-for-businesses) are far-reaching, influencing nearly every aspect of a businessâs operations. Here are two key benefits to supply chain visibility:

### Identify Risks Early

One of the most significant advantages of supply chain visibility is the **ability to identify risks early.** With clear insights into the entire supply chain, companies can pinpoint potential disruptionsâwhether from supplier delays, geopolitical events, or regulatory changesâbefore they become crises.Â

This early detection allows businesses to take proactive steps to mitigate risks, resulting in smoother operations and fewer costly disruptions. Additionally, visibility enables companies to build more resilient supply chains by identifying alternative suppliers, diversifying sourcing strategies, and addressing vulnerabilities that could threaten continuity.

### Improved Decision Making

Another key benefit is **improved decision-making**. Access to real-time data means that companies can make informed choices at every stage of the supply chain, from procurement to logistics and beyond. Decisions based on visibility are grounded in actual data, rather than assumptions or outdated information, leading to better operational efficiency and more strategic planning.Â

This data-driven approach can optimize inventory management, reduce waste, and streamline workflows, ultimately contributing to greater cost savings and operational performance. As a result, businesses with supply chain visibility are better positioned to respond to both challenges and opportunities, enhancing their overall competitiveness in a rapidly changing market.

## The Challenges of Achieving Supply Chain Visibility

While the benefits of supply chain visibility are compelling, achieving it remains a formidable undertaking for many companies. These challenges can be categorized into two camps:Â  external and internal factors.Â

### **External Challenges to Supply Chain Visibility**

External challenges to supply chain visibility are often easier to identify but can be difficult to address due to their unpredictability and the complexities involved. Here are some of the more significant external challenges companies face:

### Lack of Supplier Visibility

One of the most significant gaps in supply chain visibility is the limited insight companies have into their sub-tier suppliers. While many businesses are familiar with their direct Tier 1 suppliers, they often lack visibility into the lower levels of the supply chain where much of the risk lies. This creates a situation where a company may be unaware if a sub-tier supplier is experiencing disruptions such as a factory fire or committing human rights violations like using unethical labor practices.

Additionally, many suppliers themselves lack visibility into their own supply chains. This lack of awareness can cascade throughout the supply chain, complicating and compounding risks. For instance, if a Tier 2 supplier faces its own disruptions or is involved in non-compliant practices, the company may remain unaware until it's too late to take corrective action.

### [Read Also: Why Traditional Supplier Surveying Is No Longer Enough](https://www.z2data.com/insights/why-traditional-supplier-surveying-is-no-longer-enough)

Furthermore, even when companies do attempt to engage their suppliers, some may be unwilling to share critical information. This reluctance can stem from a variety of reasons, including concerns surrounding competitiveness, fear of negative publicity, or simply an unwillingness to disclose potential issues. The ultimate result of this deliberate absence of transparency is companies that find themselves without access to the data they need to make informed decisions, leaving them vulnerable to unforeseen risks.Â

### Evolving Regulatory Challenges

As regulations evolve, especially around environmental sustainability, human rights, and ethics, the need for greater supply chain visibility becomes even more pronounced. New and increasingly stringent regulations are forcing companies to track not just where their products are made, but how and by whom. With global markets like the European Union and the U.S. setting higher compliance standards, businesses are under pressure to capture more granular data about their supply chain operations. Failure to comply can lead to significant penalties, both financial and reputational.

### Unpredictable Supply Chain Disruptions

Natural disasters, geopolitical shifts, or even sudden supply shortages can throw a wrench into supply chain operations. While companies can map risks and prepare for certain issues, unforeseeable eventsâlike an earthquake in a key supplier regionâare impossible to predict. These disruptions can obscure real-time visibility, leaving companies scrambling to assess the full impact on their supply chains and react in a timely manner.

### Cybersecurity Risks

Cybersecurity threats have become an increasingly important concern in the digital age. A breach in a company's security systems can have far-reaching implicationsânot just for the company directly affected, but for everyone in its supply chain. Companies must be able to quickly identify the breach, assess who is affected, and communicate the necessary information to mitigate further risks. However, many companies are hesitant to disclose cybersecurity incidents due to the potential damage to their reputation, further complicating efforts to maintain transparency. The complexity of managing cybersecurity threats in a way that maintains visibility into supply chain vulnerabilities adds another layer of difficulty.

### Complex Geopolitical Factors

Geopolitical tensions, such as trade wars or shifting sanctions, present another challenge for supply chain visibility. For instance, export restrictions on critical materials, such as those on [germanium and gallium exports](https://www.z2data.com/insights/chinas-gallium-and-germanium-restrictions) by China, or the enforcement of the [Uyghur Forced Labor Prevention Act (UFLPA)](https://www.z2data.com/insights/the-complete-uflpa-entity-list-as-of-2024) by the U.S., complicate visibility efforts. In these cases, itâs not just about knowing where materials are sourced; itâs also critical to understand how political changes and emerging sanctions affect a manufacturerâs ability to source them. The rapidly changing nature of geopolitics makes it even more difficult for businesses to maintain accurate, actionable visibility into their supply chains.

### **Internal Challenges to Supply Chain Visibility**

In addition to external factors, there are a variety of internal challenges that complicate efforts to achieve supply chain visibility. These internal challenges stem primarily from how data is managed, processed, and utilized across an organization.

### Data Fragmentation

One of the primary internal challenges to supply chain visibility is data fragmentation. Companies often adopt single-purpose tools or solutions for specific aspects of supply chain management, leading to siloed data across various departments or systems. This fragmentation prevents businesses from obtaining a comprehensive, unified view of their supply chain, hampering decision-making and risk management efforts. The recent surge in AI-powered tools, while beneficial in some contexts, has only added to this complexityâespecially when these platforms lack proper integration across existing systems.

![](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/630b302c741fe7a987396dd0/673ceb3e500a645b2dcf8913_673ceae40c7ce6df3e14c681_scrm-strategy-ebook.png)

### Data Quality

While data is crucial to achieving visibility, its quality is just as important. Collecting large amounts of data is one thing, but ensuring it is accurate, up-to-date, and relevant is another. Poor-quality data leads to faulty conclusions, undermining efforts to make informed decisions about supply chain risks and opportunities. Companies need systems that not only collect vast amounts of data but also ensure that this data is verified and validated before being used in critical decision-making processes.

### Data Freshness

Even when data is accurate and well-organized, itâs of limited value if itâs outdated. In todayâs fast-paced supply chain environment, real-time visibility is essential to staying competitive. However, many companies still rely on data that is months (or more likely, years) old, limiting their ability to respond quickly to disruptions or changes in the market. To overcome this, companies must prioritize access to real-time data feeds and implement technologies that allow for constant updates and immediate access to the latest information.

### Technology Limitations

Many companies face challenges in selecting and implementing the right technological solutions to support supply chain visibility. Tracking every aspect of a supply chain in real-time, especially across multiple tiers of suppliers, is a monumental task. Even with advanced software, the infrastructure required to support real-time data collection and analysis can be prohibitively expensive for some businesses. Additionally, legacy systems that are outdated or incompatible with modern solutions can further hinder efforts to gain visibility.

### Costs

Finally, thereâs the issue of cost. Building the necessary infrastructure, investing in new technologies, and dedicating human resources to manage data collection and analysis can be expensive. For some companies, these costs can outweigh the perceived benefits, especially if theyâre already managing supply chain operations under tight budget constraints. The expense of overhauling systems, integrating new technologies, and dedicating teams to this ongoing effort may simply not be feasible, leaving supply chain visibility more of an aspirational goal than a truly achievable reality.

## Trends in Addressing Supply Chain Visibility

So, despite the significant hurdles, what are companies doing to improve supply chain visibility?

### Adoption of Technology Solutions

To address supply chain visibility challenges, many companies are increasingly adopting advanced technology solutions. Traditionally, companies would rely on supplier surveys to gather data, but this method is often static and doesnât provide real-time insights into potential risks. In contrast, technology offers a way to aggregate and vet vast amounts of data from multiple sources, supplementing supplier information with dynamic, up-to-date insights that give companies a clearer picture of their supply chain. By leveraging platforms that pull data from various sources, including FMDs, BOMs, supplier websites, and more, businesses can better track performance, disruptions, and trends across the entire supply chain.

### Adoption of AI

In addition to traditional technology solutions, artificial intelligence (AI) is becoming a key tool for enhancing supply chain visibility. By analyzing historical data, AI can forecast potential risks and disruptions, allowing businesses to better prepare and respond to challenges before they arise.Â

[Different types of AI play unique roles](https://www.z2data.com/insights/the-key-types-of-ai-and-their-roles-in-supply-chain-risk-management) across the supply chain. For instance, predictive AI models analyze past behaviors to identify emerging patterns that could indicate future disruptions. Generative AI (GenAI), which is gaining momentum, plays a crucial role in cutting down time spent analyzing data. Unlike traditional AI tools, which require human intervention to sift through aggregated data and derive insights, GenAI can autonomously generate actionable insights from the data, providing companies with faster, more informed decision-making capabilities. While AI adoption is still evolving, it continues to be a critical driver for improving visibility and mitigating risks across global supply chains.

### [Read Also: The Top Strategies for Implementing AI in Your Supply Chain](https://www.z2data.com/insights/top-strategies-for-implementing-ai-in-supply-chain)

### Improving Communication with Suppliers

While technologies like AI and risk management platforms are valuable, they can't replace the importance of strong, transparent supplier relationships. Suppliers who maintain open communication are better equipped to alert manufacturers to disruptions, allowing more time to adjust operations or find alternatives. In todayâs regulatory environment, transparent communication helps companies meet evolving standards like the Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD) and Corporate Sustainability Due Diligence Directive (CSDDD), ensuring higher compliance and reducing risks across the supply chain.

### [Read Also: Best Practices for Supplier Vetting That Will Future-Proof Your Chain](https://www.z2data.com/insights/best-practices-for-supplier-vetting-that-will-future-proof-your-chain)

### Strengthening Your Supply Chain Data

Supply chain visibility relies on accurate, comprehensive data. Companies must implement a strategy for collecting clean, actionable data, including transport records, bills of materials (BOMs), and full material declarations (FMDs) to understand all components in their products. Accessing industry platforms to track inventory and the flow of goods further enhances visibility. In short, continuous, reliable data is key to achieving effective supply chain visibility.

### Supply Chain Control Towers

Gartner defines a [supply chain control tower](https://www.gartner.com/en/articles/what-is-a-supply-chain-control-tower-and-what-s-needed-to-deploy-one) âas a concept that results in combining people, process, data, organization and technology. Control towers capture and use (close to) real-time operational data from across the business ecosystem to provide enhanced visibility and improve decision making.

In the drive to embrace new technology, companies are looking for ways to centralize and manage their operations through a unified framework. Similar to air traffic control, supply chain control towers bring together disparate technologies and data to provide a comprehensive, real-time view of supply chain health and risk. For example, companies like [Renault Group](https://www.supplychainbrain.com/articles/40609-renault-group-acquires-a-supply-chain-control-tower-to-manage-transportation-in-europe-and-beyond) are leveraging control tower solutions to enhance key areas such as transportation and logistics.

## In Summary: Supply Chain Visibility Should Be a Priority

Achieving effective supply chain visibility is no longer an optional ânice-to-haveââitâs a critical necessity for companies navigating todayâs complex business environment. Both internal and external obstacles make this goal challenging, but businesses cannot afford to delay taking action. Companies must prioritize and start planning for enhanced visibility now.Â

## What is Supply Chain Visibility?

In its simplest form, supply chain visibility collects and presents the real-time location and status of assets throughout the supply chain. This allows logistics professionals to know, in an instant, where their trucks, trailers, ships, and containers are. And, by extension, allows them to keep a closer eye on their assets and make better, faster decisions to optimize their supply chains.

The concept of supply chain visibility has been around for decades, but recent advances in technology and data science have allowed visibility software to become not only useful, but necessary for companies to keep up with global demand.

What kind of tech are we talking? Well, for example, machine learning techniques and artificial intelligence. These technologies have made it possible to use supply chain visibility to not only know where something is at any given time, but to predict exactly when it will arrive at its final destination.

## Supply Chain Visibility Terms to Know

Supply chan visibility is, by its very nature, a complex subject made up of many abstract and interconnected parts. Because of this, a few critical terms are important to know when researching the impact supply chain visibility technology might have for your organization:

### API: Application Program Interfaces

API’s are the language of the internet. API’s are how applications talk to each other, exchange information.

### ELD: Electronic Logging Devices

ELD’s are GPS-equipped record-keeping devices placed on industrial trucks and trailers to track the location and status of that asset. ELD’s were made mandatory by the U.S. government in December 2017, though they still face a slower rate of adoption in other parts of the world.

### RFID: Radio Frequency Identification

RFID is a technique for transmitting digital information about physical objects through the application of scannable tags. Bar codes are one common example of RFIDs, as are the scannable chips in credit cards and passport documents.

### Telematics

The raw data from Electronic Logging Devices, RFID’s and other visibility-related devices are collectively referred to as telematics.

### RTTVP: [Real-Time Transportation Visibility Platforms](https://www.fourkites.com/real-time-transportation-visibility-platforms/)

RTTVPs are technology tools used by shippers, carriers, and other logistics organizations to gather, analyze and interpret telematics information from a variety of sources, presenting that information in a single, actionable view.

enriched_points.point_title: Lack of visibility beyond immediate suppliers
enriched_points.expanded_content: Many companies struggle to track sub-tier suppliers, leading to significant blind spots in risk management and operational efficiency, ultimately affecting overall supply chain resilience.
Ineffective communication channels between stakeholders can hinder timely information sharing, causing delays and misunderstandings that impact supply chain performance and responsiveness.
Collaborative data sharing across the supply chain enhances transparency, enabling stakeholders to make informed decisions and respond swiftly to disruptions and market demands.
Establishing trust through open communication and transparent practices fosters stronger relationships among supply chain partners, promoting collaboration and resilience during challenging times.

Improved visibility and transparency lead to better decision-making, reduced disruptions, and increased efficiency across the supply chain, ultimately enhancing organizational performance and customer satisfaction.
